title: HTTP — How Computers Talk
subtitle: The request-response protocol of the web

sections.content:
When your browser (the client) talks to a server, they use a protocol called **HTTP** (HyperText Transfer Protocol). It's a simple back-and-forth conversation:

1. Your browser sends a **request**: "I want this page" or "Here's some data to save"
2. The server sends a **response**: "Here's the page" or "Got it, saved successfully" or "Sorry, I couldn't find that"

Every interaction on the web follows this pattern. Every page load, every form submission, every image that appears — it's all HTTP requests and responses flying back and forth.
sections.analogy: HTTP is like sending letters. You write a letter (the request) with a specific action — "Please send me your catalog" or "Here's my order form." The company writes back (the response) with what you asked for, or a note saying they couldn't help. Each letter has a clear format both sides understand.
keyPoints: HTTP = HyperText Transfer Protocol, the language of the web
Every web interaction is a request from the client and a response from the server
Requests include a **method** that says what you want to do
Responses include a **status code** that says what happened

sections.instruction: HTTP has different methods for different actions. Here are the four most common ones.

blocks.label: GET — Read data
blocks.code:
GET /api/users

"Give me the list of users."
Used when: loading a page, viewing a profile, searching

blocks.label: POST — Create data
blocks.code:
POST /api/users

"Here's a new user to add."
Used when: signing up, submitting a form, posting a comment

blocks.label: PUT — Update data
blocks.code:
PUT /api/users/42

"Update user #42 with this new info."
Used when: editing your profile, changing settings

blocks.label: DELETE — Remove data
blocks.code:
DELETE /api/users/42

"Remove user #42."
Used when: deleting your account, removing a post

sections.explanation: Think of it like CRUD: Create (POST), Read (GET), Update (PUT), Delete (DELETE). These four operations cover almost everything you do on the web.

sections.instruction: Match each HTTP status code with its meaning. You've probably seen some of these before!

sections.question: When you submit a sign-up form on a website, which HTTP method does your browser most likely use?
sections.explanation: POST is used to create new data. When you sign up, you're creating a new user account, so the browser sends a POST request with your form data to the server.

completionMessage: You now understand HTTP — the protocol that powers every interaction on the web. Requests go out, responses come back, and status codes tell you what happened. Next, we'll learn about APIs — the menus that servers offer.
